title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
About
Welcome to The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide!
Thank you for your interest in contributing to the OpenData ecosystem in R. The goal of this project is simple: make municipal open data easier to access, analyze, and teach by providing lightweight, user-friendly R packages built on a common framework.
By the end of this guide, you will have everything needed to create a fully functional OpenData package for a new city, complete with documentation, testing, a package website, and a reproducible workflow for developing and maintaining future OpenData packages.

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text: 3 Working with city_list_datasets()

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.1 Introduction
The first script to customize is city_list_datasets(). This function retrieves the metadata catalog for a city’s Open Data Portal. Rather than downloading a specific dataset, it downloads information about every dataset available on the portal.
This metadata catalog is used throughout the package. It allows users to browse available datasets, search for datasets by name, and reference datasets using either a human-readable key or the official Socrata dataset UID.
Because every Socrata portal structures its metadata catalog slightly differently, this is one of the first scripts that should be customized when creating a new package.

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.2 What Needs to Change
Although the overall workflow remains the same for every package, each city’s metadata catalog is structured slightly differently. Before moving on, verify the following components:

dataset_id: This value should be replaced with the Socrata UID of the city’s metadata catalog. This catalog contains one row for every dataset available on the portal. A list of known catalog datasets can be found on the package reference page.
type: Many Socrata portals include rows describing charts, maps, stories, or other resources in addition to datasets. When possible, filter the metadata so that only true datasets are returned. Verify both the name of the column (often type) and the value representing datasets (typically “dataset”).
name: Verify which column contains the human-readable dataset name. Most portals use either name or title, although this varies slightly between cities.
UID: The UID is the four-character-by-four-character Socrata identifier (for example erm2-nwe9) used when downloading datasets. Some metadata catalogs call this column uid, while others use id.







TipWhy make a key?



Although every Socrata dataset has a permanent UID, those identifiers are difficult to remember. For example, erm2-nwe9 is much less descriptive than parking_violations_issued. The package therefore generates a human-readable key using janitor::make_clean_names(). Users can reference datasets using either the generated key or the official UID.

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.3 Verifying Your Results
After making the necessary replacements, run the following:
city_list_dataset()
Confirm that:

the function returns a tibble
each row represents one dataset
the key column contains readable names
every row has a valid UID
duplicate UIDs have been removed
datasets can be located using keyword searches

This catches most metadata issues before moving on to the remainder of the package.

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.4 Template Values to Replace
Below is a table on the template values that need to be replaced per the specific city, and what they need to be replaced with.







Template value
Replace with




CITY_NAME
Full city name


city
Package/function prefix


CATALOG_DATASET_ID

"dataset"

KEYWORD

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.5 Common Pitfalls

Using the wrong metadata dataset.
Forgetting to filter to datasets only.
Using the wrong UID column.
Assuming the dataset name column is always called name.
Forgetting to regenerate the key column.

title: 2  Working with city_list_dataset()”
text:
3.6 Chapter Checklist
Before continuing, confirm that you have:

Replaced CITY_NAME throughout the script.
Updated the package/function prefix.
Inserted the correct metadata catalog UID.
Verified the metadata contains only datasets.
Confirmed the dataset name column.
Confirmed the UID column.
Updated the example keyword in the documentation.
Successfully ran city_list_datasets().
Confirmed the output contains the expected metadata.
Documentation examples run successfully.

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
3.1 Introduction
With city_list_datasets() complete, we can now customize city_pull_dataset(). This is the primary function users will interact with when using the package. Its purpose is to download individual datasets from the city’s Open Data Portal.
flowchart TD
A[User provides key or UID] --> B[Search metadata catalog]
B --> C[Retrieve dataset UID]
C --> D[Build SoQL query]
D --> E[Download dataset]
E --> F[Return tibble]
Unlike city_list_datasets(), which retrieves metadata describing every dataset on the portal, city_pull_dataset() retrieves the actual records from a single dataset selected by the user.

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
Tipcity_pull_dataset() depends on city_list_datasets()



This function first looks up the requested dataset in the metadata catalog returned by city_list_datasets(). It then retrieves the corresponding Socrata UID before downloading the dataset itself.

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
3.2 What Needs to Change

3.2.1 Code Changes
Very little of the underlying function needs to change from city to city.
Verify the following:

UID column
The function assumes the metadata catalog contains a column named uid. If your city’s metadata catalog instead uses id, update every reference to .data$uid accordingly.



3.2.2 Documentation Changes
Most of the customization for city_pull_dataset() occurs in the documentation examples rather than the function itself. Be sure to update:

every placeholder dataset UID
every placeholder dataset key
every placeholder field name
every placeholder filter value
every placeholder date field
every ORDER BY example
every example so it executes successfully on the city’s Open Data Portal

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
3.3 Choosing an Example Dataset
Select one dataset from the portal that:

is publicly accessible
contains at least several hundred rows
has one or more character fields suitable for filtering
contains at least one date or datetime column
is unlikely to disappear in the near future

Use this dataset consistently throughout the documentation examples.






TipUse a real dataset in every example



Avoid creating artificial examples whenever possible.
Using a real dataset allows users to copy and paste examples directly into R, making it much easier to verify that the package is working correctly.

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
3.4 Verifying Your Results
After making the necessary replacements, verify that:

datasets can be pulled by key
datasets can be pulled by UID
filters return the expected rows
date filtering works correctly
ordering works correctly
invalid dataset names produce an informative error
documentation examples execute successfully

title: 3  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
3.6 Common Pitfalls
When customizing city_pull_dataset(), the most common issues are:

Forgetting to update the example dataset UID after choosing a new dataset.
Choosing an example dataset that does not contain a date column, causing the date-filtering examples to fail.
Using placeholder field names (such as FILTER_FIELD or DATE_FIELD) instead of real column names.
Using filter values that no longer exist in the example dataset.
Selecting a dataset that is updated infrequently or removed from the portal, causing examples to break over time.
Updating the documentation but forgetting to update the corresponding examples in the function documentation.
Assuming the metadata catalog uses a uid column when it actually uses id.

text:

Updated all package function prefixes.

Updated every example key.
Updated every filter field.

Updated every date field.

Successfully downloaded a dataset using its key.
Successfully downloaded a dataset using its UID.
Verified the documentation examples run successfully.
Verified clean_names = TRUE works.
Verified coerce_types = TRUE works.

title: 4  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
4.1 Introduction
city_any_dataset() serves as a fallback when a dataset cannot be located through city_list_datasets(). Rather than searching the package’s metadata catalog, this function downloads data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint.
flowchart TD
A[User provides JSON endpoint] --> B[Validate URL]
B --> C[Send API request]
C --> D[Download JSON]
D --> E[Post-process data]
E --> F[Return tibble]
In most cases, users should prefer city_pull_dataset(), since it provides readable dataset keys and additional convenience features. However, city_any_dataset() is useful for newly published datasets, datasets omitted from the metadata catalog, or any valid Socrata JSON endpoint.

title: 4  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
TipWhen should I use city_any_dataset()?



Use city_pull_dataset() whenever the dataset is available in the package catalog.
Use city_any_dataset() only when you already have a Socrata JSON endpoint or when the dataset is not available through city_list_datasets().

title: 4  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
4.2 What Needs to Change
Very little of the underlying function changes from city to city.
The primary customizations are:

updating package-specific function names
updating package-specific internal helper names
replacing the example Socrata domain
replacing the example dataset UID

title: 4  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
4.3 Verifying Your Results
After making the necessary replacements, verify that:

a valid JSON endpoint downloads successfully
invalid URLs produce informative errors
column names are cleaned when clean_names = TRUE
type coercion behaves as expected
documentation examples execute successfully

4.5 Common Pitfalls
When customizing city_any_dataset(), the most common issues are:

title: 5  Working With utils_request.R
text:
5.1 Introduction
Unlike the previous chapters, this file does not define functions that package users call directly. Instead, it contains the internal helper functions responsible for constructing API requests, validating user inputs, downloading data, and post-processing responses.
flowchart TD
A[User calls package function]
--> B[Input validation]
--> C[Build endpoint]
--> D[Build SoQL query]
--> E[Download JSON]
--> F[Post-process data]
--> G[Return tibble]
Nearly every exported function in the package relies on these helpers. By centralizing this logic in a single script, improvements and bug fixes only need to be made once rather than being duplicated throughout the package.

title: 5  Working With utils_request.R
text:
TipThese are internal helper functions



The functions in utils_request.R are internal utilities and are not intended to be called directly by package users.
Instead, they provide the shared functionality used by city_list_datasets(), city_pull_dataset(), and city_any_dataset().

title: 5  Working With utils_request.R
text:
5.2 What Needs to Change
Most helper functions can be reused without modification. However, verify the following:

.city_endpoint() uses the correct Socrata domain.
All internal helper names use the package prefix.
Package-facing error messages reference the correct city.
Remove reviewer comments that were specific to the template development process.

The remaining helper functions are intentionally generic and should not require modification unless you are changing the overall architecture of th e package.

title: 5  Working With utils_request.R
text:
5.3 Understanding the Helper Functions







Helper
Purpose




.city_endpoint()
Constructs the Socrata JSON endpoint for a dataset


.city_validate_limit()
Validates the limit argument


.city_validate_timeout()
Validates request timeout values


.city_validate_filters()
Validates user-supplied filters


.city_add_filters()
Converts filters into SoQL equality clauses


.city_add_where()
Appends raw SoQL WHERE conditions


.city_get_json()
Executes the API request and parses the JSON response


.city_dataset_request()
Coordinates validation, querying, downloading, and post-processing


.city_postprocess()
Cleans column names and optionally coerces data types


.city_build_date_where()
Constructs date-based SoQL filters

title: 5  Working With utils_request.R
text:
5.6 Common Pitfalls
When customizing utils_request.R, the most common issues are:

Forgetting to update the Socrata domain in .city_endpoint().
Leaving template package prefixes (such as .city_*) unchanged.
Forgetting to update city names in user-facing error messages.
Removing helper functions that are still referenced elsewhere in the package.
Modifying shared helper functions without testing all exported functions.
Leaving reviewer comments from the template in the finished package.

title: 1  Open Data Packages
text:
1.1 Introduction
The package template described throughout this guide is designed for cities that use the Socrata Open Data Platform. Because Socrata provides a standardized API across portals, the same package architecture can be reused with only a handful of city-specific modifications.
This chapter lists major U.S. cities with Socrata-based Open Data portals and provides the two most important values needed when creating a new package:

title: 1  Open Data Packages
text:
1.2 Before You Begin
Before starting a new package, verify that:

The city uses a Socrata-powered Open Data portal.
The metadata catalog dataset is publicly accessible.
Dataset JSON endpoints follow the standard /resource/<uid>.json format.
API requests do not require authentication for the datasets you intend to use.

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
Why OpenData Packages?
Many cities publish enormous amounts of valuable public data through Socrata-powered Open Data portals. While these datasets are freely available, accessing them typically requires users to understand APIs, JSON endpoints, and the Socrata Query Language (SoQL).
OpenData packages remove much of that technical overhead by allowing users to interact with public datasets through familiar R functions. This makes civic data more approachable for students, researchers, journalists, educators, and anyone interested in working with public information.

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
Why Is This Important?
Every new OpenData package lowers the barrier to working with public data. Whether the package is used in a classroom, a newsroom, a research lab, or by residents exploring their own communities, each contribution helps make civic data more accessible and reproducible.

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
History
In Fall 2025, while teaching Reproducible Research Using R in the M.S. in Psychological Research program at Brooklyn College (CUNY), I found myself facing an unexpected problem.
Initially, the course relied on traditional teaching datasets such as mtcars. While useful for learning R, these datasets lacked personal relevance for many students. To make coursework more engaging, the course shifted toward using live datasets from the NYC Open Data Portal, allowing students to investigate questions about their own communities using continuously updated public data.
To remove the technical barriers associated with working with APIs, the nycOpenData package was developed. Rather than requiring students to learn API syntax before analyzing data, the package allowed them to begin exploring NYC datasets immediately using simple R functions.
Since then, nycOpenData has undergone peer review and been accepted into the rOpenSci ecosystem. The same architecture has since been used to develop additional OpenData packages for other Socrata-powered portals.

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
Current Landscape
The OpenData ecosystem continues to grow. Currently, the following packages are available on CRAN:

nycOpenData
nysOpenData
mtaOpenData
laOpenData
chiOpenData

Together, these packages provide convenient access to tens of billions of rows of publicly available data spanning transportation, public safety, health, education, housing, the environment, and many other domains.
As additional packages are developed, this ecosystem will continue expanding to make municipal Open Data more accessible across the United States.
Each package shares a common architecture, allowing contributors to develop additional city-specific packages using the same reproducible workflow described throughout this guide.

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
Who Is This Guide For?
This guide is intended for anyone interested in creating a new OpenData package for a Socrata-powered Open Data portal.
While some familiarity with R package development is helpful, this guide does not attempt to teach:

R programming
API development
SoQL
General R package development

Instead, it focuses specifically on adapting the existing OpenData framework to a new city, allowing contributors to build consistent, maintainable packages without starting from scratch.
This guide is especially useful for:

R package developers
Students contributing to the OpenData ecosystem
Civic technologists
Researchers working with municipal data
Contributors interested in expanding the OpenData package collection

title: The OpenData Package Developer’s Guide
text:
How to Use This Guide
The chapters in this guide are designed to be completed sequentially.
For each script you will:

Understand its role within the package.
Learn what needs to be customized.
Verify that your changes work correctly.
Review common pitfalls.
Complete a checklist before moving to the next chapter.

Following this workflow ensures that every package built from the template follows the same architecture, quality standards, and user experience—making the OpenData ecosystem easier to maintain, expand, and use.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.1 Creating a New Package
Before customizing the OpenData template, you’ll need a working R package project connected to GitHub. This chapter walks through creating a new package, connecting it to a GitHub repository, and preparing it to use the OpenData template.
In RStudio, select File → New Project. Then:

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
Choose New Directory.
Select R Package.
Enter a package name.
Choose the directory where the package should be created.
Click Create Project.


1.1.1 Making Things Easier:
If Git is installed on your computer, you may also see an option to Create a git repository. Checking this box will automatically initialize Git for the project, allowing you to skip Step 2 below.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.2 Connecting to GitHub
Version control is an essential part of modern R package development. Throughout this guide, Git and GitHub are used to track changes, collaborate with others, host package source code, and publish package websites using GitHub Pages.
Fortunately, only a small subset of Git functionality is needed to build an OpenData package.

1.2.1 Step 1: Create a GitHub Repository
Begin by logging into GitHub and creating a new repository.
When creating the repository:

Give the repository the same name as your package (for example, nycOpenData).
Keep the repository Public if you plan to share the package.
Do not initialize the repository with a README, .gitignore, or license if you already created the package locally. These files already exist in your package.

Once the repository has been created, copy its URL.
For example:
https://github.com/USERNAME/nycOpenData.git


1.2.2 Step 2: Initialize Git
Open your package project in RStudio.
If Git was not enabled when the project was created, initialize it from the Terminal:
git init
Then verify Git is active:
git status


1.2.3 Step 3: Connect the Local Package to GitHub
Add the GitHub repository as the remote origin.
git remote add origin https://github.com/USERNAME/nycOpenData.git
Verify the connection:
git remote -v
You should now see the GitHub repository listed as the remote.


1.2.4 Step 4: Make Your First Commit
Stage every file:
git add .
Commit them:
git commit -m "Initial package template"


1.2.5 Step 5: Push to GitHub
Push the package to GitHub
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
After refreshing GitHub, your package should now appear online.


1.2.6 Daily Workflow
After the initial setup, the Git workflow is very simple.
Whenever you finish making changes:
git add .
git commit -m "Describe what changed"
git push
Most OpenData package development only requires these three commands.
At this point, your package structure is complete and connected to GitHub. The final step is to replace the default package files with the OpenData template.


1.2.7 Updating an Existing Package
If you already cloned a package from GitHub and someone else has made changes, download the latest updates before beginning your work.
git pull


1.2.8 Quick Reference



Command
Purpose




git init
Initialize Git for a project


git status
View changed files


git add .
Stage all changes


git commit -m "message"
Save a snapshot of your changes


git push
Upload changes to GitHub


git pull
Download the latest changes


git remote -v
View connected GitHub repository





1.2.9 Common Pitfalls

Forgetting to commit changes before pushing.
Forgetting to pull the latest changes before starting work.
Creating the GitHub repository with a README when the package already contains one.
Accidentally working in the wrong repository.
Using vague commit messages such as "update" instead of describing what actually changed.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.3 Using the Template
Once the package has been created and connected to GitHub, download the latest version of the OpenData template.
The template contains the core files needed to build an OpenData package, including:

city_list_datasets.R
city_pull_dataset.R
city_any_dataset.R
utils_request.R
tests
documentation
package metadata

Copy these files into your package, replacing the placeholder files created by RStudio.
After copying the template, run:
devtools::document()
to regenerate the package documentation before beginning the customization chapters.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.4 Chapter Checklist
Before continuing, confirm that you have:

Created a new R package.

Copied the template into your package.

title: 2  Choosing a City

title: 2  Choosing a City
text:
2.2 Before You Begin
Before starting a new package, verify that:

The city uses a Socrata-powered Open Data portal.
The metadata catalog dataset is publicly accessible.
Dataset JSON endpoints follow the standard /resource/<uid>.json format.
API requests do not require authentication for the datasets you intend to use.

title: 3  Working with city_list_datasets()
text:
3.1 Introduction
The first script to customize is city_list_datasets(). This function retrieves the metadata catalog for a city’s Open Data Portal. Rather than downloading a specific dataset, it downloads information about every dataset available on the portal.
This metadata catalog is used throughout the package. It allows users to browse available datasets, search for datasets by name, and reference datasets using either a human-readable key or the official Socrata dataset UID.
Because every Socrata portal structures its metadata catalog slightly differently, this is one of the first scripts that should be customized when creating a new package.

title: 3  Working with city_list_datasets()
text:
3.2 What Needs to Change
Although the overall workflow remains the same for every package, each city’s metadata catalog is structured slightly differently. Before moving on, verify the following components:

dataset_id: This value should be replaced with the Socrata UID of the city’s metadata catalog. This catalog contains one row for every dataset available on the portal. A list of known catalog datasets can be found on the package reference page.
type: Many Socrata portals include rows describing charts, maps, stories, or other resources in addition to datasets. When possible, filter the metadata so that only true datasets are returned. Verify both the name of the column (often type) and the value representing datasets (typically “dataset”).
name: Verify which column contains the human-readable dataset name. Most portals use either name or title, although this varies slightly between cities.
UID: The UID is the four-character-by-four-character Socrata identifier (for example erm2-nwe9) used when downloading datasets. Some metadata catalogs call this column uid, while others use id.







TipWhy make a key?



Although every Socrata dataset has a permanent UID, those identifiers are difficult to remember. For example, erm2-nwe9 is much less descriptive than parking_violations_issued. The package therefore generates a human-readable key using janitor::make_clean_names(). Users can reference datasets using either the generated key or the official UID.

title: 3  Working with city_list_datasets()
text:
3.3 Verifying Your Results
After making the necessary replacements, run the following:
city_list_dataset()
Confirm that:

the function returns a tibble
each row represents one dataset
the key column contains readable names
every row has a valid UID
duplicate UIDs have been removed
datasets can be located using keyword searches

This catches most metadata issues before moving on to the remainder of the package.

title: 4  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
4.1 Introduction
With city_list_datasets() complete, we can now customize city_pull_dataset(). This is the primary function users will interact with when using the package. Its purpose is to download individual datasets from the city’s Open Data Portal.
flowchart TD
A[User provides key or UID] --> B[Search metadata catalog]
B --> C[Retrieve dataset UID]
C --> D[Build SoQL query]
D --> E[Download dataset]
E --> F[Return tibble]
Unlike city_list_datasets(), which retrieves metadata describing every dataset on the portal, city_pull_dataset() retrieves the actual records from a single dataset selected by the user.

title: 4  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
Tipcity_pull_dataset() depends on city_list_datasets()



This function first looks up the requested dataset in the metadata catalog returned by city_list_datasets(). It then retrieves the corresponding Socrata UID before downloading the dataset itself.

title: 4  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
4.2 What Needs to Change

4.2.1 Code Changes
Very little of the underlying function needs to change from city to city.
Verify the following:

UID column
The function assumes the metadata catalog contains a column named uid. If your city’s metadata catalog instead uses id, update every reference to .data$uid accordingly.



4.2.2 Documentation Changes
Most of the customization for city_pull_dataset() occurs in the documentation examples rather than the function itself. Be sure to update:

every placeholder dataset UID
every placeholder dataset key
every placeholder field name
every placeholder filter value
every placeholder date field
every ORDER BY example
every example so it executes successfully on the city’s Open Data Portal

title: 4  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
4.3 Choosing an Example Dataset
Select one dataset from the portal that:

is publicly accessible
contains at least several hundred rows
has one or more character fields suitable for filtering
contains at least one date or datetime column
is unlikely to disappear in the near future

Use this dataset consistently throughout the documentation examples.






TipUse a real dataset in every example



Avoid creating artificial examples whenever possible.
Using a real dataset allows users to copy and paste examples directly into R, making it much easier to verify that the package is working correctly.

ordering works correctly
invalid dataset names produce an informative error
documentation examples execute successfully

title: 4  Working with city_pull_dataset()
text:
4.6 Common Pitfalls
When customizing city_pull_dataset(), the most common issues are:

Forgetting to update the example dataset UID after choosing a new dataset.
Choosing an example dataset that does not contain a date column, causing the date-filtering examples to fail.
Using placeholder field names (such as FILTER_FIELD or DATE_FIELD) instead of real column names.
Using filter values that no longer exist in the example dataset.
Selecting a dataset that is updated infrequently or removed from the portal, causing examples to break over time.
Updating the documentation but forgetting to update the corresponding examples in the function documentation.
Assuming the metadata catalog uses a uid column when it actually uses id.

title: 5  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
5.1 Introduction
city_any_dataset() serves as a fallback when a dataset cannot be located through city_list_datasets(). Rather than searching the package’s metadata catalog, this function downloads data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint.
flowchart TD
A[User provides JSON endpoint] --> B[Validate URL]
B --> C[Send API request]
C --> D[Download JSON]
D --> E[Post-process data]
E --> F[Return tibble]
In most cases, users should prefer city_pull_dataset(), since it provides readable dataset keys and additional convenience features. However, city_any_dataset() is useful for newly published datasets, datasets omitted from the metadata catalog, or any valid Socrata JSON endpoint.

title: 5  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
TipWhen should I use city_any_dataset()?



Use city_pull_dataset() whenever the dataset is available in the package catalog.
Use city_any_dataset() only when you already have a Socrata JSON endpoint or when the dataset is not available through city_list_datasets().

title: 5  Working with city_any_dataset()
text:
5.2 What Needs to Change
Very little of the underlying function changes from city to city.
The primary customizations are:

updating package-specific function names
updating package-specific internal helper names
replacing the example Socrata domain
replacing the example dataset UID

title: 6  Working With utils_request.R
text:
6.1 Introduction
Unlike the previous chapters, this file does not define functions that package users call directly. Instead, it contains the internal helper functions responsible for constructing API requests, validating user inputs, downloading data, and post-processing responses.
flowchart TD
A[User calls package function]
--> B[Input validation]
--> C[Build endpoint]
--> D[Build SoQL query]
--> E[Download JSON]
--> F[Post-process data]
--> G[Return tibble]
Nearly every exported function in the package relies on these helpers. By centralizing this logic in a single script, improvements and bug fixes only need to be made once rather than being duplicated throughout the package.

title: 6  Working With utils_request.R
text:
TipThese are internal helper functions



The functions in utils_request.R are internal utilities and are not intended to be called directly by package users.
Instead, they provide the shared functionality used by city_list_datasets(), city_pull_dataset(), and city_any_dataset().

title: 6  Working With utils_request.R
text:
6.2 What Needs to Change
Most helper functions can be reused without modification. However, verify the following:

.city_endpoint() uses the correct Socrata domain.
All internal helper names use the package prefix.
Package-facing error messages reference the correct city.
Remove reviewer comments that were specific to the template development process.

The remaining helper functions are intentionally generic and should not require modification unless you are changing the overall architecture of th e package.

text:

Helper
Purpose

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.1 Package Metadata
At this point, the package’s core functionality is complete. However, a high-quality R package consists of much more than its R scripts. This chapter walks through the remaining files that should be reviewed before submitting the package to CRAN or sharing it with others.
There are several documents that make up the metadata regarding the package itself. These include the following.

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.1.1 Description
The DESCRIPTION file serves as the package’s identity card. It contains metadata used by CRAN, GitHub, and other R tooling to describe the package and its dependencies.

7.1.1.1 Commonly Updated Fields

Package – The name of your package.
Title – A short, descriptive title explaining the purpose of the package.
Version – The current version number of the package, updated with each release.
Authors – The names and roles of the package authors and contributors.
Description – A more detailed explanation of what the package does and who it is for.
URL – Links to the package website, GitHub repository, or other relevant resources.
BugReports – The URL where users can report issues or bugs.
Imports – Packages that your package depends on to function correctly.
Suggests – Packages used for optional features such as testing, examples, or vignettes.



7.1.1.2 Fields That Are Usually Left Unchanged

Type – Specifies the package type, which will almost always be "Package".
Encoding – Defines the text encoding used by the package, typically "UTF-8".
RoxygenNote – Records the version of roxygen2 used to generate the documentation.
Depends – Lists packages that users must attach when loading your package, though Imports is generally preferred.
VignetteBuilder – Specifies the package used to build the package vignettes.
Config/testthat/edition – Indicates the version of testthat used for the package’s testing framework.




7.1.2 Namespace
This is a file that is usually generated automatically by roxygen2 (when you run devtools::document()). You should almost never edit this file manually.

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.2 Documentation

7.2.1 License
This is telling the community how they can work with this package. Unless you are changing the package’s licensing model, this file is typically created once and rarely modified.


7.2.2 News
This is a place to put any news regarding any changes/updates that have been made to the package. This should most likely be updated with every version update. For example, if you update the package from 0.1.0 to 0.1.1, then the changes should be described in News.


7.2.3 README
The README is often the first thing users see when visiting the package’s GitHub repository. A good README should explain what the package does, how to install it, and include several working examples.
For a package, there will be two README files:

README.Rmd
README.md

The .Rmd file is a traditional R Markdown file, while the .md file is the rendered version of the .Rmd file. Typically, you would edit the .Rmd file, render the output, and the .md file would automatically update.
Although not required by CRAN, a README is strongly recommended because it serves as the primary landing page for users visiting the package on GitHub.
Additionally, this will become critical if creating a webpage, such as pkgdown, for the package.


7.2.4 Vignettes
Vignettes are long-form tutorials that demonstrate how to use your package in realistic workflows. Unlike individual function documentation, which explains a single function, vignettes show how multiple functions work together to solve a problem.
For an OpenData package, a vignette is an excellent place to demonstrate a complete workflow, such as:

Listing available datasets with city_list_datasets()
Downloading a dataset using city_pull_dataset()
Filtering observations
Working with date ranges
Performing a simple analysis or visualization

Well-written vignettes help new users become productive more quickly and provide examples that are often more comprehensive than those included in individual function documentation.
By default, vignettes are stored in the package’s vignettes/ directory and are automatically included in the pkgdown website.
It is strongly encouraged all OpenData packages have at least one vignette.






TipKeep vignettes reproducible



Because vignettes are intended to be run by users, avoid examples that depend on temporary datasets, hard-coded file paths, or values that frequently change. Whenever possible, use stable datasets and limit the number of rows downloaded so the vignette runs quickly while still demonstrating the package’s functionality.




7.2.5 pkgdown Website
One of the easiest ways to make an R package look professional is by creating a pkgdown website. A pkgdown website automatically converts your package documentation into a searchable website, making it easier for users to browse functions, read vignettes, and learn how to use your package.
When hosted through GitHub Pages, the website serves as the public-facing homepage for your package. Rather than navigating individual help files, users can access reference documentation, installation instructions, articles, and examples from a single location.
A typical pkgdown website includes:

A homepage generated from the package README.md
Reference pages for every exported function
Vignettes and tutorials
News and release notes from NEWS.md
Links to the package source code, issue tracker, and documentation

For many users, the pkgdown website will be their first interaction with your package. Spending time to create a clear homepage and well-written function documentation can greatly improve the usability of the package.






TipRebuild your website after making changes



Whenever you update your package documentation, README, or vignettes, remember to rebuild the pkgdown website so the online documentation stays synchronized with the package. If you are using pkgdown, this can typically be done with:

pkgdown::build_site()

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.4 Common Pitfalls
Making a package is a lot of work. Here are some of the things to look out for:

Editing README.md instead of README.Rmd
Editing NAMESPACE manually
Forgetting to run document()
Forgetting to bump the version
Forgetting to update NEWS.md
Using stale URLs
Forgetting to rebuild pkgdown







TipDon’t try to perfect everything at once



Most package development is iterative.
Get the package working first, then improve the documentation, website, examples, and testing over time.

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.5 Before Submitting
The final steps before making the package public, whether to GitHub or CRAN, are as follows.

7.5.1 Testing
Before publishing your package, run the following commands in order:

devtools::document(): Regenerates documentation and updates NAMESPACE.
devtools::test(): Runs the package’s automated tests.
devtools::check(): Simulates CRAN checks to identify problems before submission.



7.5.2 Before Publishing
In the cases when you are re-publishing your package after having made some updates, here are things to look out for:

DESCRIPTION updated
README rebuilt
Documentation rebuilt
NEWS updated
Website rebuilt
Tests pass
devtools::check() passes
No NOTES
Version number updated
Package website deployed (if using pkgdown)
GitHub repository updated
CRAN comments prepared (if submitting to CRAN)
Vignettes rebuilt and tested.



7.5.3 Final Workflow





flowchart LR
A[Customize Template]
--> B[Test Package]
--> C[Update Documentation]
--> D[Build Website]
--> E[Run devtools::check()]
--> F[Publish]








7.5.4 Submitting to CRAN
When everything is all said and done, and you are ready to submit to cran, you can use the following command:

devtools::release()

devtools::release() guides you through a series of checks before preparing the package for submission. Once the checks pass, you’ll be prompted to answer a few questions and then directed to submit the package to CRAN. After submission, you’ll receive a confirmation email from CRAN, and a reviewer will either accept the package or contact you with any requested changes.

title: 7  Finalizing Your Package
text:
7.6 Congratulations
Congratulations! At this point, you have customized the package template, updated the documentation, tested the package, and prepared it for publication.
Whether you choose to share your package through GitHub, submit it to CRAN, or contribute it to a larger ecosystem such as rOpenSci, you now have a reproducible framework for creating Open Data packages that can be maintained and expanded over time.
The remaining chapters provide additional reference material and resources that may be helpful as you continue developing your package.

Thank you for contributing to the OpenData ecosystem. Every new package makes public data more accessible to researchers, students, educators, journalists, and the communities these datasets represent.

title: Appendix A — Template: city_list_dataset()
text:
#' List available CITY_NAME Open Data datasets
#'
#' Retrieves a live catalog of CITY_NAME Open Data datasets from the CITY_NAME
#' Open Data Portal catalog table (`CATALOG_DATASET_ID`) and returns dataset
#' metadata that can be used with [city_pull_dataset()].
#'
#' The returned tibble includes both the official Socrata dataset `uid` and a
#' human-readable `key`. The `uid` is the stable identifier used by the CITY_NAME
#' Open Data Portal and Socrata API, while the `key` is generated from the
#' dataset name using [janitor::make_clean_names()] to make datasets easier to
#' reference in R code, especially in classroom and reproducible research
#' settings.
#'
#' Most users will begin by calling `city_list_datasets()`, searching the
#' returned catalog for a dataset of interest, and then passing either the `key`
#' or `uid` to [city_pull_dataset()].
#'
#' @return A tibble containing available CITY_NAME Open Data datasets. Important
#'   columns include:
#'   \describe{
#'     \item{key}{A human-readable dataset key generated from the dataset name.}
#'     \item{uid}{The official Socrata dataset identifier used by CITY_NAME Open
#'       Data.}
#'     \item{name}{The dataset name from the CITY_NAME Open Data catalog.}
#'   }
#'   Additional metadata columns from the CITY_NAME Open Data catalog may also
#'   be returned.
#'
#' @examples
#' if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {
#'   catalog <- city_list_datasets()
#'
#'   # View available datasets
#'   catalog
#'
#'   # Search for datasets containing a keyword
#'   catalog[
#'     grepl("KEYWORD", catalog$name, ignore.case = TRUE),
#'     c("key", "uid", "name")
#'   ]
#' }
#'
#' @importFrom rlang .data
#' @export
city_list_datasets <- function() {
  .city_catalog_tbl()
}

.city_catalog_tbl <- function() {
  raw <- .city_dataset_request(
    dataset_id = "CATALOG_DATASET_ID",
    limit = 50000,
    timeout_sec = 30,
    clean_names = TRUE,
    coerce_types = FALSE
  )

  raw |>
    dplyr::filter(.data$type == "dataset") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      key = janitor::make_clean_names(.data$name)
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(.data$uid), nzchar(.data$uid)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(.data$uid, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
    dplyr::relocate("key", "uid", "name")
}

title: Appendix B — Template: city_pull_dataset()
text:
#' Pull a CITY_NAME Open Data dataset
#'
#' Downloads a dataset from the CITY_NAME Open Data Socrata API using either a
#' human-readable catalog `key` or the official Socrata dataset `uid` returned by
#' [city_list_datasets()].
#'
#' When a catalog `key` is supplied, `city_pull_dataset()` first retrieves the
#' live CITY_NAME Open Data catalog to look up the corresponding Socrata `uid`,
#' then sends a second request to download the dataset itself. Supplying a `uid`
#' directly is more stable and avoids ambiguity, while keys are provided for
#' readability and classroom-friendly workflows.
#'
#' Dataset keys are generated from dataset names using
#' [janitor::make_clean_names()]. Because keys are derived from live catalog
#' metadata, Socrata UIDs are the most stable identifiers.
#'
#' @param dataset A single dataset `key` or Socrata dataset `uid` from
#'   [city_list_datasets()]. For example, a key may look like
#'   `"example_dataset_name"`, while a UID may look like `"abcd-1234"`.
#' @param limit Number of rows to retrieve. Defaults to 10,000.
#' @param filters Optional named list of exact-match filters. Each list name
#'   should be a field name in the dataset, and each value should be the value
#'   or values to match. Vector values are translated into SQL-style `IN`
#'   conditions in the generated SoQL query. For example,
#'   `filters = list(FILTER_FIELD = c("VALUE_1", "VALUE_2"))` returns rows where
#'   `FILTER_FIELD` is either `"VALUE_1"` or `"VALUE_2"`.
#' @param date Optional single date used to match all records from that day.
#'   Requires `date_field`.
#' @param from Optional start date, inclusive. Requires `date_field`.
#' @param to Optional end date, exclusive. Requires `date_field`.
#' @param date_field Optional date or datetime column to use with `date`,
#'   `from`, or `to`. This must be supplied when any date filter is used. Users
#'   can identify available date columns by inspecting the dataset on the
#'   CITY_NAME Open Data Portal or by pulling a small sample with `limit`.
#' @param where Optional raw SoQL `WHERE` clause for advanced filtering. SoQL is
#'   the Socrata Query Language used by CITY_NAME Open Data. If `date`, `from`,
#'   or `to` are also supplied, their generated conditions are combined with
#'   `where` using `AND`.
#' @param order Optional raw SoQL `ORDER BY` clause, such as
#'   `"DATE_FIELD DESC"`.
#' @param timeout_sec Request timeout in seconds. Defaults to 30.
#' @param clean_names Logical. If `TRUE`, column names are converted to
#'   snake_case using [janitor::clean_names()]. Defaults to `TRUE`.
#' @param coerce_types Logical. If `TRUE`, the package attempts lightweight,
#'   heuristic-based type coercion after downloading the data. Columns are
#'   converted only when at least 95 percent of non-missing values can be parsed
#'   as the target type. This helps avoid unsafe conversions when source data are
#'   inconsistent.
#'
#' @return A tibble containing rows from the requested CITY_NAME Open Data
#'   dataset.
#'
#' @details
#' `city_pull_dataset()` is designed for common catalog-based workflows. For
#' arbitrary Socrata JSON endpoints that are not included in the package catalog,
#' use [city_any_dataset()].
#'
#' The `filters` argument is intended for simple exact-match filtering. For more
#' complex conditions, use the `where` argument with raw SoQL syntax.
#'
#' Internally, filter field names are wrapped in `TRIM()` when constructing SoQL
#' queries to reduce mismatches caused by leading or trailing whitespace in
#' source data.
#'
#' Type coercion is intentionally conservative. When `coerce_types = TRUE`, the
#' package attempts to infer common R column types from the API response, but
#' columns with inconsistent values may remain character columns.
#'
#' Datetime coercion is also conservative. Timezone offsets and sub-second
#' precision may not always be preserved during automatic parsing, and columns
#' with inconsistent datetime formats may remain character columns.
#'
#' @examples
#' if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {
#'   # Pull by human-readable key
#'   city_pull_dataset("example_dataset_name", limit = 3)
#'
#'   # Pull by Socrata UID
#'   city_pull_dataset("abcd-1234", limit = 3)
#'
#'   # Filter to one value
#'   city_pull_dataset(
#'     "abcd-1234",
#'     limit = 3,
#'     filters = list(FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1")
#'   )
#'
#'   # Filter to multiple values
#'   city_pull_dataset(
#'     "abcd-1234",
#'     limit = 10,
#'     filters = list(FILTER_FIELD = c("VALUE_1", "VALUE_2"))
#'   )
#'
#'   # Date filtering
#'   city_pull_dataset(
#'     "abcd-1234",
#'     from = "2023-01-01",
#'     to = "2024-01-01",
#'     date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
#'     limit = 100
#'   )
#'
#'   # Advanced filtering with raw SoQL
#'   city_pull_dataset(
#'     "abcd-1234",
#'     where = "FILTER_FIELD = 'VALUE_1' AND SECOND_FIELD = 'VALUE_2'",
#'     order = "DATE_FIELD DESC",
#'     limit = 100
#'   )
#' }
#'
#' @export
city_pull_dataset <- function(dataset,
                              limit = 10000,
                              filters = list(),
                              date = NULL,
                              from = NULL,
                              to = NULL,
                              date_field = NULL,
                              where = NULL,
                              order = NULL,
                              timeout_sec = 30,
                              clean_names = TRUE,
                              coerce_types = TRUE) {

  # dataset validation
  if (!is.character(dataset) ||
      length(dataset) != 1 ||
      is.na(dataset) ||
      !nzchar(dataset)) {
    stop(
      "`dataset` must be a single, non-missing character value.",
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  cat_tbl <- .city_catalog_tbl()

  # sanity checks on required columns
  req <- c("key", "uid")
  missing_req <- setdiff(req, names(cat_tbl))

  if (length(missing_req) > 0) {
    stop(
      paste0(
        "Internal error: catalog missing required column(s): ",
        paste(missing_req, collapse = ", "),
        "."
      ),
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  row <- cat_tbl[
    cat_tbl$key == dataset | cat_tbl$uid == dataset,
    ,
    drop = FALSE
  ]

  if (nrow(row) == 0) {
    stop(
      paste0(
        "Unknown dataset: '", dataset, "'. ",
        "Use `city_list_datasets()` to see available keys and UIDs."
      ),
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  if (nrow(row) > 1) {
    stop(
      paste0(
        "Multiple catalog matches found for '", dataset, "'. ",
        "Please use the dataset UID instead."
      ),
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  uid <- row$uid[[1]]

  # require date_field if user supplies date filters
  if ((!is.null(date) || !is.null(from) || !is.null(to)) &&
      (is.null(date_field) ||
       !is.character(date_field) ||
       length(date_field) != 1 ||
       !nzchar(date_field))) {
    .city_abort(
      paste0(
        "If `date`, `from`, or `to` are supplied, you must also ",
        "provide a single non-empty `date_field`."
      )
    )
  }

  date_where <- .city_build_date_where(
    date_field = date_field,
    date = date,
    from = from,
    to = to
  )

  where_final <- where

  if (!is.null(date_where)) {
    if (is.null(where_final) || !nzchar(where_final)) {
      where_final <- date_where
    } else {
      where_final <- paste0(
        "(",
        where_final,
        ") AND (",
        date_where,
        ")"
      )
    }
  }

  .city_dataset_request(
    dataset_id = uid,
    limit = limit,
    filters = filters,
    order = order,
    where = where_final,
    timeout_sec = timeout_sec,
    clean_names = clean_names,
    coerce_types = coerce_types
  )
}

title: Appendix C — Template: city_any_dataset()
text:
#' Pull any CITY_NAME Open Data dataset from a Socrata JSON endpoint
#'
#' Downloads data from any CITY_NAME Open Data Socrata JSON endpoint and returns
#' the result as a tibble. This function is useful for datasets that are not
#' included in the curated catalog returned by [city_list_datasets()].
#'
#' CITY_NAME Open Data datasets have Socrata JSON endpoints that usually follow
#' this pattern:
#'
#' `https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/<dataset_uid>.json`
#'
#' For example, a dataset with the Socrata UID `"abcd-1234"` would have the JSON
#' endpoint:
#'
#' `https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json`
#'
#' Users can find a dataset's UID from the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal URL, the API
#' documentation page for the dataset, or the output of [city_list_datasets()].
#'
#' @param json_link A single Socrata dataset JSON endpoint URL, such as
#'   `"https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json"`.
#' @param limit Number of rows to retrieve. Defaults to 10,000.
#' @param timeout_sec Request timeout in seconds. Defaults to 30.
#' @param clean_names Logical. If `TRUE`, column names are converted to
#'   snake_case using [janitor::clean_names()]. Defaults to `TRUE`.
#' @param coerce_types Logical. If `TRUE`, the package attempts lightweight,
#'   heuristic-based type coercion after downloading the data. Columns are
#'   converted only when at least 95 percent of non-missing values can be parsed
#'   as the target type. This helps avoid unsafe conversions when source data are
#'   inconsistent.
#'
#' @return A tibble containing rows from the requested CITY_NAME Open Data
#'   endpoint.
#'
#' @details
#' `city_any_dataset()` bypasses the package catalog and sends a request directly
#' to the supplied JSON endpoint. Unlike [city_pull_dataset()], it does not look
#' up defaults such as catalog keys, date fields, or default ordering.
#'
#' This function is intended for direct endpoint access. For catalog-based
#' workflows using readable keys or Socrata UIDs, use [city_pull_dataset()].
#'
#' @examples
#' # Examples that hit the live CITY_NAME Open Data API are guarded so CRAN
#' # checks do not fail when the network is unavailable or slow.
#' if (interactive() && curl::has_internet()) {
#'   # Build a JSON endpoint from a Socrata UID
#'   uid <- "abcd-1234"
#'   endpoint <- paste0("https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/", uid, ".json")
#'
#'   out <- try(city_any_dataset(endpoint, limit = 3), silent = TRUE)
#'   if (!inherits(out, "try-error")) {
#'     head(out)
#'   }
#' }
#'
#' @export
city_any_dataset <- function(json_link,
                             limit = 10000,
                             timeout_sec = 30,
                             clean_names = TRUE,
                             coerce_types = TRUE) {

  if (!is.character(json_link) || length(json_link) != 1 || is.na(json_link)) {
    stop("`json_link` must be a single, non-missing character URL.", call. = FALSE)
  }

  if (!grepl("\\.json($|\\?)", json_link)) {
    stop(
      "`json_link` must be a Socrata JSON endpoint ending in .json.",
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }

  limit <- .city_validate_limit(limit)
  timeout_sec <- .city_validate_timeout(timeout_sec)

  query_list <- list("$limit" = limit)

  data <- .city_get_json(
    json_link,
    query_list,
    timeout_sec = timeout_sec
  )

  out <- tibble::as_tibble(data, .name_repair = "minimal")

  # Optional post-processing pipeline
  out <- .city_postprocess(
    out,
    clean_names = clean_names,
    coerce_types = coerce_types
  )

  out
}

title: Appendix F — Template: README.Rmd
text:
G.1 How cityOpenData Works
The package provides a streamlined interface to the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal’s Socrata API.
Internally, cityOpenData:

retrieves metadata from the live CITY_NAME Open Data catalog
constructs parameterized HTTP requests
downloads JSON responses from Socrata endpoints
converts results into tidy tibble outputs
optionally cleans column names
optionally performs conservative type coercion

Most workflows begin with city_list_datasets(), which retrieves a live catalog of datasets available through the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal.
Datasets can then be downloaded using either:

a human-readable catalog key
the official Socrata dataset UID, such as "abcd-1234"

The human-readable key is designed to improve readability and usability, while the UID is the stable identifier used by the Socrata platform.

title: Appendix F — Template: README.Rmd
text:
G.2 Core Functions
The package provides three primary functions:

city_list_datasets() retrieves a live catalog of available CITY_NAME Open Data datasets, including human-readable keys, Socrata UIDs, names, and other available metadata.
city_pull_dataset() downloads cataloged datasets using either a human-readable key or Socrata UID, with support for filtering, ordering, date ranges, optional column-name cleaning, and optional type coercion.
city_any_dataset() downloads data directly from a valid Socrata JSON endpoint without requiring the dataset to appear in the package catalog.

Datasets retrieved through city_pull_dataset() support arguments including:

limit
filters
date
from
to
date_field
where
order
clean_names
coerce_types

All functions return tibble outputs.
Advanced users may also provide raw SoQL conditions through the where argument.
SoQL, or Socrata Query Language, is the query syntax used by Socrata-powered Open Data portals. Additional information is available from the Socrata developer documentation.

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette

city_list_datasets() for browsing available datasets
city_pull_dataset() for downloading datasets using a catalog key or Socrata UID
city_any_dataset() for downloading data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.2 Listing Available Datasets
The first step in a typical workflow is to use city_list_datasets() to retrieve the live CITY_NAME Open Data catalog.
catalog <- city_list_datasets()

catalog
The returned catalog includes information about the datasets available through the portal. Two especially important columns are:

key, a human-readable dataset identifier generated from the dataset name
uid, the official Socrata dataset identifier

You can search the catalog for datasets containing a keyword.
catalog |>
  filter(grepl("KEYWORD", name, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
  select(key, uid, name)
Replace KEYWORD with a useful search term related to the example dataset selected for the package.

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.3 Pulling a Dataset
The primary way to download data is with city_pull_dataset().
A dataset can be requested using either its human-readable catalog key or its official Socrata UID.

G.3.1 Pulling by UID
example_data_uid <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 5
)

example_data_uid


G.3.2 Pulling by Key
example_data_key <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "example_dataset_name",
  limit = 5
)

example_data_key
Both calls should return data from the same dataset.






TipKeys and UIDs



Dataset keys are easier to read, while Socrata UIDs are more stable.
For reproducible research and long-term workflows, using the official Socrata UID is generally recommended.

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.4 Filtering Data
The filters argument can be used for simple exact-match filtering.
filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 25,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1"
  )
)

filtered_data
You can confirm that the filter worked by inspecting the unique values in the selected field.
filtered_data |>
  distinct(FILTER_FIELD)
Multiple values can also be supplied.
filtered_multiple <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 50,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = c("VALUE_1", "VALUE_2")
  )
)

filtered_multiple
Multiple fields can be combined within the same filter list.
filtered_combination <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 50,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1",
    SECOND_FIELD = "SECOND_VALUE"
  )
)

filtered_combination

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.5 Filtering by Date
If the example dataset contains a date or datetime field, records can be filtered using from, to, and date_field.
date_filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  from = "2023-01-01",
  to = "2024-01-01",
  date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
  limit = 100
)

date_filtered_data
The from date is inclusive, while the to date is exclusive.
A single day can also be requested using the date argument.
single_day_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  date = "2023-06-01",
  date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
  limit = 100
)

single_day_data

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
section: G.6 Pulling Data from Any Socrata Endpoint
text:
G.6 Pulling Data from Any Socrata Endpoint
The preferred workflow is to use city_list_datasets() together with city_pull_dataset().
However, when a dataset is not available in the package catalog, city_any_dataset() can download data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint.
CITY_NAME Open Data endpoints typically follow this structure:
https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/<dataset_uid>.json
For example:
https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json
The endpoint can then be supplied directly to city_any_dataset().
endpoint_data <- city_any_dataset(
  json_link = "https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json",
  limit = 5
)

endpoint_data






TipWhich function should you use?



Use city_pull_dataset() when the dataset is available through city_list_datasets().
Use city_any_dataset() when you already have a valid Socrata JSON endpoint or when the dataset is not included in the package catalog.

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.7 Example Analysis
Once the data have been downloaded, they can be analyzed using standard R tools.
The following example counts the number of records in a categorical field.
category_summary <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 500
) |>
  filter(!is.na(CATEGORY_FIELD)) |>
  count(CATEGORY_FIELD, sort = TRUE)

category_summary
The results can then be visualized.
{text category-plot, fig.alt="Bar chart showing the most frequent categories in the example CITY_NAME Open Data dataset.", fig.cap="Most frequent categories in the example CITY_NAME Open Data dataset.", fig.height=5, fig.width=7} category_summary |>   slice_head(n = 10) |>   ggplot(     aes(       x = n,       y = reorder(CATEGORY_FIELD, n)     )   ) +   geom_col() +   theme_minimal() +   labs(     title = "Most Frequent Categories",     x = "Number of Records",     y = "Category"   )
This example demonstrates the complete workflow from discovering a dataset to downloading, filtering, summarizing, and visualizing it.

title: Appendix G — Template: Vignette
text:
G.8 Summary
The cityOpenData package provides a consistent interface for working with data from the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal.
In this vignette, you learned how to:

browse available datasets using city_list_datasets()
download datasets by key or UID using city_pull_dataset()

# cityOpenData <img src="man/figures/cityOpenData_hex.png" alt="cityOpenData logo" width="72" align="right" />

[![CRAN status](https://www.r-pkg.org/badges/version/cityOpenData)](https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=cityOpenData)
[![CRAN downloads](https://cranlogs.r-pkg.org/badges/grand-total/cityOpenData?color=blue)](https://r-pkg.org/pkg/cityOpenData)
[![Lifecycle: stable](https://img.shields.io/badge/lifecycle-stable-brightgreen.svg)](https://lifecycle.r-lib.org/articles/stages.html)
[![Project Status: Active](https://www.repostatus.org/badges/latest/active.svg)](https://www.repostatus.org/#active)
[![R-CMD-check](https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cityOpenData/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cityOpenData/actions/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml)

`cityOpenData` provides a lightweight R interface to the
[CITY_NAME Open Data Portal](https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/).

The package allows users to search, filter, and download datasets from the
CITY_NAME Open Data Portal directly into R without manually constructing API
queries, handling JSON responses, or performing type conversion.

Designed for students, educators, researchers, journalists, civic
technologists, and analysts, `cityOpenData` reduces the technical overhead
required to begin working with municipal Open Data while preserving access to
the underlying Socrata infrastructure.

---

## How `cityOpenData` Works

The package provides a streamlined interface to the CITY_NAME Open Data
Portal's Socrata API.

Internally, `cityOpenData`:

- retrieves metadata from the live CITY_NAME Open Data catalog
- constructs parameterized HTTP requests
- downloads JSON responses from Socrata endpoints
- converts results into tidy tibble outputs
- optionally cleans column names
- optionally performs conservative type coercion

Most workflows begin with `city_list_datasets()`, which retrieves a live catalog
of datasets available through the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal.

Datasets can then be downloaded using either:

- a human-readable catalog `key`
- the official Socrata dataset UID, such as `"abcd-1234"`

The human-readable key is designed to improve readability and usability, while
the UID is the stable identifier used by the Socrata platform.

## Core Functions

The package provides three primary functions:

- `city_list_datasets()` retrieves a live catalog of available CITY_NAME Open
  Data datasets, including human-readable keys, Socrata UIDs, names, and other
  available metadata.

- `city_pull_dataset()` downloads cataloged datasets using either a
  human-readable key or Socrata UID, with support for filtering, ordering, date
  ranges, optional column-name cleaning, and optional type coercion.

- `city_any_dataset()` downloads data directly from a valid Socrata JSON
  endpoint without requiring the dataset to appear in the package catalog.

Datasets retrieved through `city_pull_dataset()` support arguments including:

- `limit`
- `filters`
- `date`
- `from`
- `to`
- `date_field`
- `where`
- `order`
- `clean_names`
- `coerce_types`

All functions return tibble outputs.

Advanced users may also provide raw SoQL conditions through the `where`
argument.

SoQL, or Socrata Query Language, is the query syntax used by Socrata-powered
Open Data portals. Additional information is available from the
[Socrata developer documentation](https://dev.socrata.com/docs/queries/).

---

## Installation

### Install from CRAN

```r
install.packages("cityOpenData")
```

### Install the development version from GitHub

```r
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cityOpenData")
```

Alternatively:

```r
# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cityOpenData")
```

---

## Example

```{text example, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(cityOpenData)
library(dplyr)

# Browse available datasets
catalog <- city_list_datasets()

# Search for datasets containing a keyword
catalog |>
  filter(grepl("KEYWORD", name, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
  select(key, uid, name)

# Pull a dataset using its UID
example_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 100
)

# Pull the same dataset using its catalog key
example_data_by_key <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "example_dataset_name",
  limit = 100
)

# Pull filtered data
filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 100,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1"
  )
)
```

The `filters` argument accepts a named list and automatically constructs the
corresponding SoQL filtering conditions.

Multiple values may be supplied for one field:

```{text multiple-values, eval=FALSE}
filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 100,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = c("VALUE_1", "VALUE_2")
  )
)
```

Multiple fields may also be combined:

```{text multiple-fields, eval=FALSE}
filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 100,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1",
    SECOND_FIELD = "SECOND_VALUE"
  )
)
```

Date filtering is available for datasets containing date or datetime fields:

```{text date-filter, eval=FALSE}
date_filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  from = "2023-01-01",
  to = "2024-01-01",
  date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
  limit = 100
)
```

---

## Accessing Any Socrata Endpoint

When a dataset is not available through `city_list_datasets()`, it can be
downloaded directly using `city_any_dataset()`.

```{text any-dataset, eval=FALSE}
endpoint_data <- city_any_dataset(
  json_link = "https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json",
  limit = 100
)
```

Use `city_pull_dataset()` for catalog-based workflows and
`city_any_dataset()` when working directly with a Socrata JSON endpoint.

---

## Learn by Example

A complete introductory workflow is available in the package vignette:

```r
vignette("getting-started", package = "cityOpenData")
```

The vignette demonstrates how to:

- browse the dataset catalog
- download data using a key or UID
- filter records
- work with date ranges
- access direct JSON endpoints
- perform a simple analysis

---

## Package Website

Complete documentation is available on the package website:

<https://YOUR_PACKAGE_WEBSITE/>

The website includes:

- function reference pages
- installation instructions
- introductory articles
- vignettes
- release notes

---

To run the package tests locally:

```

```

```

```

## Contributing

Contributions are welcome.

To report a bug, request a feature, or suggest an improvement, open an issue on

<https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/cityOpenData/issues>

Pull requests are also welcome. Before submitting a pull request, please ensure

---

## Author

**YOUR_NAME**

Email: [YOUR_EMAIL](mailto:YOUR_EMAIL)  
GitHub: [@YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME](https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME)  
ORCID: [YOUR_ORCID](https://orcid.org/YOUR_ORCID)

---

## Maintenance

Because the package retrieves metadata dynamically from the live CITY_NAME Open
Data catalog, newly published datasets may become available without requiring a
package update.

Package updates may still be required when the portal changes its catalog
structure, dataset metadata fields, or API behavior.

---

## Disclaimer

`cityOpenData` is an independent project and is not affiliated with, endorsed
by, or maintained by CITY_NAME or the organization responsible for the CITY_NAME
Open Data Portal.

---
title: "Getting Started with CITY_NAME Open Data"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
author: "YOUR_NAME"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Getting Started with CITY_NAME Open Data}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{text, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

```{text setup}
library(cityOpenData)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
```

## Introduction

Welcome to the `cityOpenData` package, an R package designed to provide convenient access to the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal.

The package provides a streamlined interface for discovering and downloading datasets from CITY_NAME Open Data. It helps bridge the gap between raw Socrata API endpoints and tidy data analysis in R.

The package provides three primary functions:

- `city_list_datasets()` for browsing available datasets
- `city_pull_dataset()` for downloading datasets using a catalog key or Socrata UID
- `city_any_dataset()` for downloading data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint

## Listing Available Datasets

The first step in a typical workflow is to use `city_list_datasets()` to retrieve the live CITY_NAME Open Data catalog.

```{text list-datasets}
catalog <- city_list_datasets()

catalog
```

The returned catalog includes information about the datasets available through the portal. Two especially important columns are:

- `key`, a human-readable dataset identifier generated from the dataset name
- `uid`, the official Socrata dataset identifier

You can search the catalog for datasets containing a keyword.

```{text search-catalog}
catalog |>
  filter(grepl("KEYWORD", name, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
  select(key, uid, name)
```

Replace `KEYWORD` with a useful search term related to the example dataset selected for the package.

## Pulling a Dataset

The primary way to download data is with `city_pull_dataset()`.

A dataset can be requested using either its human-readable catalog key or its official Socrata UID.

### Pulling by UID

```{text pull-by-uid}
example_data_uid <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 5
)

example_data_uid
```

### Pulling by Key

```{text pull-by-key}
example_data_key <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "example_dataset_name",
  limit = 5
)

example_data_key
```

Both calls should return data from the same dataset.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Keys and UIDs

Dataset keys are easier to read, while Socrata UIDs are more stable.

For reproducible research and long-term workflows, using the official Socrata UID is generally recommended.
:::

## Filtering Data

The `filters` argument can be used for simple exact-match filtering.

```{text filter-one-value}
filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 25,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1"
  )
)

filtered_data
```

You can confirm that the filter worked by inspecting the unique values in the selected field.

```{text verify-filter}
filtered_data |>
  distinct(FILTER_FIELD)
```

Multiple values can also be supplied.

```{text filter-multiple-values}
filtered_multiple <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 50,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = c("VALUE_1", "VALUE_2")
  )
)

filtered_multiple
```

Multiple fields can be combined within the same filter list.

```{text filter-multiple-fields}
filtered_combination <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 50,
  filters = list(
    FILTER_FIELD = "VALUE_1",
    SECOND_FIELD = "SECOND_VALUE"
  )
)

filtered_combination
```

## Filtering by Date

If the example dataset contains a date or datetime field, records can be filtered using `from`, `to`, and `date_field`.

```{text date-filter}
date_filtered_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  from = "2023-01-01",
  to = "2024-01-01",
  date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
  limit = 100
)

date_filtered_data
```

The `from` date is inclusive, while the `to` date is exclusive.

A single day can also be requested using the `date` argument.

```{text single-date-filter}
single_day_data <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  date = "2023-06-01",
  date_field = "DATE_FIELD",
  limit = 100
)

single_day_data
```

## Pulling Data from Any Socrata Endpoint

The preferred workflow is to use `city_list_datasets()` together with `city_pull_dataset()`.

However, when a dataset is not available in the package catalog, `city_any_dataset()` can download data directly from a Socrata JSON endpoint.

CITY_NAME Open Data endpoints typically follow this structure:

```text
https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/<dataset_uid>.json
```

For example:

```text
https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json
```

The endpoint can then be supplied directly to `city_any_dataset()`.

```{text any-dataset}
endpoint_data <- city_any_dataset(
  json_link = "https://SOCRATA_DOMAIN/resource/abcd-1234.json",
  limit = 5
)

endpoint_data
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Which function should you use?

Use `city_pull_dataset()` when the dataset is available through `city_list_datasets()`.

Use `city_any_dataset()` when you already have a valid Socrata JSON endpoint or when the dataset is not included in the package catalog.
:::

## Example Analysis

Once the data have been downloaded, they can be analyzed using standard R tools.

The following example counts the number of records in a categorical field.

```{text summarize-example}
category_summary <- city_pull_dataset(
  dataset = "abcd-1234",
  limit = 500
) |>
  filter(!is.na(CATEGORY_FIELD)) |>
  count(CATEGORY_FIELD, sort = TRUE)

category_summary
```

The results can then be visualized.

```{text category-plot, fig.alt="Bar chart showing the most frequent categories in the example CITY_NAME Open Data dataset.", fig.cap="Most frequent categories in the example CITY_NAME Open Data dataset.", fig.height=5, fig.width=7}
category_summary |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  ggplot(
    aes(
      x = n,
      y = reorder(CATEGORY_FIELD, n)
    )
  ) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Most Frequent Categories",
    x = "Number of Records",
    y = "Category"
  )
```

This example demonstrates the complete workflow from discovering a dataset to downloading, filtering, summarizing, and visualizing it.

## Summary

The `cityOpenData` package provides a consistent interface for working with data from the CITY_NAME Open Data Portal.

In this vignette, you learned how to:

- browse available datasets using `city_list_datasets()`
- download datasets by key or UID using `city_pull_dataset()`
- filter data using fields, values, and dates
- access a Socrata JSON endpoint using `city_any_dataset()`
- perform a simple analysis and visualization

These functions allow users to focus on analysis rather than manually constructing API requests.

## How to Cite

If you use this package for research or educational purposes, cite it using the package citation returned by:

```{text citation, eval=FALSE}
citation("cityOpenData")
```
